moduleStablecoins.algorithmic.closing: This design can work while confidence holds, but it removes the safety net that collateral-backed models have.
moduleStablecoins.algorithmic.li1: No independent collateral. Backing comes from confidence in the system itself, not from assets held elsewhere.
moduleStablecoins.algorithmic.li2: Reflexive design. The mechanism that defends the peg depends on the volatile token holding value, which itself depends on confidence in the peg.
moduleStablecoins.algorithmic.li3: Death spiral risk. If confidence breaks, both tokens can fall together, since minting more of the volatile token to defend the peg only works while someone is still willing to buy it.
moduleStablecoins.algorithmic.p1: Algorithmic stablecoins try to hold their peg through code and incentives instead of holding real collateral. A common design pairs the stablecoin with a second, volatile token that absorbs the difference.
moduleStablecoins.algorithmic.p2: When the stablecoin trades below its peg, the protocol lets users burn it in exchange for newly minted volatile token, reducing the stablecoin's supply and, in theory, pushing its price back up.
moduleStablecoins.algorithmic.title: Algorithmic Stablecoins
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def1: The target value a stablecoin is designed to maintain, usually a fiat currency.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def2: The value of backing assets held relative to the stablecoins issued.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def3: An event where a stablecoin's market price moves meaningfully away from its target peg.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def4: A report (not a full audit) confirming a stablecoin issuer's reserves at a point in time.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def5: A stablecoin that uses code and incentives, rather than collateral, to hold its peg.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def6: Buying an asset where it's cheap and selling where it's expensive, the mechanism that keeps a stablecoin's price close to its peg.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def7: Backing a loan or a stablecoin with collateral worth more than the amount issued, to absorb price volatility.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def8: The collateralization ratio below which a position can be automatically closed to protect the system.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.title: Glossary
moduleStablecoins.hero.description: Stablecoins try to hold a steady value in a volatile market. This module explains the three ways they attempt it.
moduleStablecoins.hero.stat1.label: Difficulty
moduleStablecoins.hero.stat2.label: Duration
moduleStablecoins.hero.stat3.label: Format
moduleStablecoins.hero.stat4.label: Sections
moduleStablecoins.hero.title: Stablecoins
moduleStablecoins.historicalDepegs.card1: An algorithmic stablecoin that lost its peg during a period of large withdrawals; its companion token's price collapsed at the same time, and the peg was never recovered.
moduleStablecoins.historicalDepegs.card2: Briefly traded near $0.88 after news that part of its reserves were held at a bank that had just failed. It returned to $1.00 within days once the issuer confirmed reserves remained accessible.
moduleStablecoins.historicalDepegs.closi: The same word, depeg, describes a temporary wobble in one case and a permanent collapse in the other. What backs the token is what determines which one you're looking at.
moduleStablecoins.historicalDepegs.p1: Two well-documented cases show how differently a depeg can play out depending on what actually backs a stablecoin.
moduleStablecoins.historicalDepegs.title: Historical Depegs
moduleStablecoins.introduction.p1: Module 05 introduced stablecoins briefly as one of DeFi's core building blocks. This lesson slows down and looks at what actually keeps a stablecoin's price steady, and why "stable" is a design goal, not a guarantee.
moduleStablecoins.introduction.p2: A stablecoin is a token engineered to hold a steady value, usually pegged to a fiat currency like the US dollar. How that peg is maintained differs enormously between designs, and that difference is exactly where the risk lives.
moduleStablecoins.introduction.p3: By the end of this lesson you'll be able to tell fiat-backed, crypto-backed and algorithmic stablecoins apart, understand what a reserve attestation actually proves, and recognize the warning signs that tend to precede a depeg.
moduleStablecoins.introduction.title: Introduction
moduleStablecoins.meta.title: Stablecoins — Nodra Academy
If collateral value falls below the required ratio, the position can be liquidated automatically to protect the system, a mechanic covered in Module 06 — Lending.
Crypto-backed stablecoins can't rely on a stable asset as collateral, so they compensate by requiring more collateral value than the stablecoin they issue.
If $100 of ETH is deposited to mint $70 of a stablecoin, the position can absorb roughly a 30% drop in ETH's price before the collateral is no longer sufficient to back what was issued.
moduleStablecoins.pegMechanisms.closing: This mechanism only works if redemption and minting are actually reliable, if an issuer freezes redemptions, the arbitrage loop breaks and the peg can drift indefinitely.
moduleStablecoins.pegMechanisms.p1: A peg isn't enforced by a rule, it's maintained by arbitrage: whenever the price drifts away from the target, someone has a financial incentive to trade it back.
moduleStablecoins.pegMechanisms.p2: If a fiat-backed stablecoin trades below $1, arbitrageurs can buy it cheaply and redeem it for $1 from the issuer, pushing demand (and price) back up. If it trades above $1, they can mint new tokens for $1 and sell them for a profit, pushing price back down.
moduleStablecoins.pegMechanisms.title: How the Peg Is Maintained
moduleStablecoins.quiz.intro: Check your understanding of this lesson before moving on. Incorrect answers point you straight back to the relevant section.
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q1: 1. What determines which of the three main stablecoin designs a token uses?
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q1.feedbackIncorr: Not quite, review Types of Stablecoins and try again.
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q1o1: A. The blockchain it is issued on
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q1o2: B. What actually backs it, fiat reserves, crypto collateral, or code and incentives
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q1o3: C. Whether it pays yield to holders
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q1o4: D. How many exchanges list it
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q2: 2. Why does arbitrage help keep a fiat-backed stablecoin near its $1 peg?
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q2.feedbackIncorr: Not quite, review How the Peg Is Maintained and try again.
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q2o1: A. Because the issuer manually sets the price each day
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q2o2: B. Because traders profit by pushing the price back toward $1, buying when it's cheap and minting/selling when it's expensive
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q2o3: C. Because exchanges refuse to list it below $1
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q2o4: D. Because governments require it by law
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q3: 3. If $100 of collateral backs $70 of a stablecoin, roughly how much can the collateral's price fall before it's no longer sufficient?
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q3.feedbackIncorr: Not quite, review Over-Collateralization and try again.
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q4: 4. What does a reserve attestation actually confirm?
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q4.feedbackIncorr: Not quite, review Reserve Attestation and try again.
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q4o1: A. That the stablecoin can never lose its peg
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q4o2: B. That reserves of a reported value existed on a specific date
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q4o3: C. That the issuer has been fully audited every year
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q4o4: D. That reserves are held in the safest possible assets
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q5: 5. What makes algorithmic stablecoins riskier than collateral-backed ones?
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q5.feedbackIncorr: Not quite, review Algorithmic Stablecoins and try again.
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q5o1: A. They are always more expensive to use
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q5o2: B. Their peg depends on ongoing confidence rather than assets held elsewhere
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q5o3: C. They cannot be traded on exchanges
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q5o4: D. They are illegal in most countries
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q6: 6. What was the key difference between UST's 2022 depeg and USDC's 2023 depeg?
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q6.feedbackIncorr: Not quite, review Historical Depegs and try again.
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q6o1: A. UST recovered quickly while USDC never did
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q6o2: B. USDC was backed by reserves that were temporarily inaccessible, not gone, while UST's backing mechanism collapsed entirely
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q6o3: C. Both were caused by the same root issue
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q6o4: D. Neither actually lost its peg
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q7: 7. Which of these is a genuine red flag for a stablecoin?
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q7.feedbackIncorr: Not quite, review Risks & Red Flags and try again.
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q7o2: B. A yield far above what comparable, lower-risk options pay
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q7o3: C. Being redeemable 1:1 with the issuer
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q7o4: D. Having deep liquidity across major exchanges
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q8: 8. What is the single most useful question to ask before trusting a stablecoin's peg?
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q8.feedbackIncorr: Not quite, review Risks & Red Flags and try again.
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q8o1: A. How many social media followers the project has
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q8o3: C. How long ago it launched
moduleStablecoins.quiz.q8o4: D. What color its logo is
moduleStablecoins.quiz.title: Quiz
moduleStablecoins.reserveAttestation.clo: Treat attestations as a useful data point, not as proof that a stablecoin can never break its peg.
moduleStablecoins.reserveAttestation.not: Ongoing solvency, the quality or liquidity of the reserves, or that reserves have not changed since.
moduleStablecoins.reserveAttestation.p1: Fiat-backed issuers regularly publish a reserve attestation, a report from an accounting firm confirming that reserves of a certain value existed on a specific date.
moduleStablecoins.reserveAttestation.p2: An attestation is not the same as a full audit. It confirms a snapshot, not that reserves are always fully backing every token in circulation, nor how those reserves are invested or how quickly they could be accessed.
moduleStablecoins.reserveAttestation.pro: That reported reserves existed, in some form, on the date it covers.
moduleStablecoins.risks.intro: Not every stablecoin carries the same risk, but a few warning signs are worth checking before relying on one.
moduleStablecoins.risks.li1: Opaque reserves. An issuer that won't publish regular attestations, or discloses vague reserve composition, is harder to trust by default.
moduleStablecoins.risks.li2: Reflexive backing. Any design where the peg depends on confidence in another token issued by the same protocol carries the algorithmic risk covered above.
moduleStablecoins.risks.li3: Thin liquidity. A stablecoin that's hard to buy or sell at its peg price in size is more likely to slip during stress, even if it's fully backed.
moduleStablecoins.risks.li4: Regulatory exposure. Reserves held at a single bank or jurisdiction can become temporarily inaccessible for reasons that have nothing to do with the stablecoin's own design.
moduleStablecoins.risks.li5: Unsustainable yield. A stablecoin offering yield well above what fiat savings or DeFi lending normally pay is often subsidizing that rate from somewhere, and that somewhere matters.
moduleStablecoins.risks.title: Risks & Red Flags
moduleStablecoins.summary.p1: Stablecoins hold their value through one of three broad designs, fiat reserves, crypto over-collateralization, or algorithmic incentives, and each trades decentralization for a different kind of risk.
moduleStablecoins.summary.p2: A reserve attestation is a useful signal but not a guarantee, over-collateralization absorbs volatility but isn't limitless, and algorithmic designs remove the collateral safety net entirely. The single most useful habit from this lesson is asking what actually backs a stablecoin before treating its peg as guaranteed.
moduleStablecoins.summary.title: Summary
moduleStablecoins.toc.title: Contents
moduleStablecoins.types.card1.text: Backed 1:1 by cash and cash-equivalents held by a company, off-chain. Examples: USDC, USDT.
moduleStablecoins.types.card1.title: Fiat-Backed
moduleStablecoins.types.card2.text: Backed by other cryptocurrencies locked as collateral, usually over-collateralized to absorb price swings. Example: DAI.
moduleStablecoins.types.card2.title: Crypto-Backed
moduleStablecoins.types.card3.text: Backed mainly by code and market incentives rather than collateral, expanding and contracting supply to defend the peg.
moduleStablecoins.types.card3.title: Algorithmic
moduleStablecoins.types.closing: Each model answers the same question, what stands behind this token, in a very different way.
moduleStablecoins.types.p1: Nearly every stablecoin in use today falls into one of three broad designs, each trading off decentralization, capital efficiency and risk differently.
moduleStablecoins.types.title: Types of Stablecoins
